source: Learning Rsync - rsync demo 01: SSH-based rsync: authentication, pull/download, push/upload, and permissions

questions.q: What do rsync's two authentication methods use?
options: SSH auth uses the Linux system user/password; the rsync protocol uses its own (virtual) users
SSH uses LDAP, rsync uses Kerberos
questions.why: SSH verification uses GNU/Linux system accounts; the rsync protocol uses non-system virtual users (like vsftpd).

questions.q: Which is the pull/download form (fetch a remote file to the local machine)?
questions.why: For a pull the source is remote (user@host:path) and the destination is local.
For a push the source is local and the destination is remote.

questions.q: The server's SSH port is not the default 22. How do you tell rsync?
questions.why: -e sets the remote shell, e.g. rsync -avz -e 'ssh -p 2222' ...

questions.q: A push fails with 'mkstemp ... Permission denied'. What is the likely cause?
options: The target directory is not writable by the rsync user
questions.why: The /rsync/ directory lacked write permission for the testrsync user.

questions.q: How was that permission problem fixed in the demo?
options: setfacl -m u:testrsync:rwx /rsync/  (grant the user rwx via an ACL)
questions.why: An ACL granting testrsync rwx on the directory (setfacl -m u:testrsync:rwx) fixed the write failure.
